Market Volatility (VIX) Signal
← Back to dashboard

Market Volatility (VIX) Signal

VIX market volatility signal: tracking investor fear and uncertainty.

As of 2036-10-01

Gemini Summary

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a VIX of 17.48 and an MVS_z of 0.03, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift. (1)
  • The signal has transitioned from a STRESSED state in late March to a NORMAL regime as volatility momentum decelerates. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Improving; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Low.

Methodology Applied:

  • A VIX between 16 and 20 or an MVS_z between 0 and 0.50 defines the NORMAL regime, representing standard variance pricing. (1)
  • Falling values in the volatility composite imply a transition toward market expansion and risk-on sentiment. (1)
  • VIX levels below 16 combined with negative MVS_z indicate a CALM environment supportive of carry trades. (1)
  • Market Volatility (VIX) Signal data used through 2026-04-17. (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • Primary drivers include a sharp decline in short-term volatility momentum (d5_z: -0.78) and medium-term stabilization (d20_z: -2.51). (1)
  • The MVS_score has fallen from a peak of 65.33 (March 6) to 12.54, indicating significant cooling in hedging demand. (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: A reversal in VIX above 20 or MVS_z exceeding 0.50 would trigger an ELEVATED regime shift. (1)
  • The signal shows high persistence in its current downward trajectory toward the CALM threshold. (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant base case: Volatility normalization continues as realized variance aligns with lower implied levels.
  • Upside risk: Transition to CALM regime if VIX sustains levels below 16.00.
  • Downside risk: Re-entry into STRESSED regime triggered by an exogenous shock causing VIX to spike above 30.00.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Tactical (weeks): The signal measures 30-day forward-implied expectations and reacts to short-horizon hedging flows. (1)
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; the signal effectively gauges equity sentiment but requires confirmation from liquidity and credit spreads. (1)

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the risk sentiment dimension, specifically reflecting the cost of equity downside protection. (1)
  • The implied economic mechanism is an expansion of risk appetite as the volatility risk premium compresses. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with USD strength and credit spreads; divergence between VIX and credit would signal internal market tension. (1)

Regime Context:

  • Transitioned from STRESSED to NORMAL over the last 15 trading days. (1)
  • Direction of change: Stabilising and improving toward the CALM boundary. (1)

Model Limitations:

  • Snapshots may miss intramonth stress bursts; absolute thresholds are sensitive to structural market regime shifts. (1)

Data & References:

  • Market Volatility (VIX) Signal: Latest observation 2026-04-17. (1)
  • Most influential datapoints: VIX (17.48) and MVS_z (0.03). (1)
  • Public datasets for depth: Realized S&P 500 volatility (30-day) and CBOE Equity Put/Call Ratio.

VIX index: market volatility and investor sentiment.
